title: Dental implants vs bridges — which is better for a missing tooth?
excerpt: Both replace missing teeth, but they work very differently. Here is an honest comparison to help you decide.

content.text: When you lose a tooth, you have two main fixed options: a dental implant or a dental bridge. Both can restore the appearance of your smile and your ability to eat comfortably, but they differ significantly in how they work, how long they last, and what they mean for the health of your surrounding teeth.
How a dental bridge works

A dental bridge fills the gap left by a missing tooth by anchoring an artificial tooth to the teeth on either side. Those adjacent teeth must be prepared — meaning they are permanently reduced in size and crowned — to support the bridge. This is a well-established solution and can look excellent, but it involves altering healthy teeth that might otherwise remain untouched for decades.
How a dental implant works

A dental implant replaces the root of the missing tooth with a titanium post that is placed directly into the jawbone. Once the implant integrates with the bone — a process called osseointegration — a crown is placed on top. The result is a fully independent tooth replacement that does not involve the adjacent teeth at all.
The key differences

The most important difference is that implants preserve the adjacent teeth completely, while a bridge requires them to be permanently altered. Implants also preserve the jawbone — when a tooth root is missing, the bone beneath it gradually resorbs. An implant stimulates the bone just like a natural root, preventing this loss. Bridges do not.
Which lasts longer?

A well-placed implant can last a lifetime with proper care. Bridges typically last 10 to 15 years before needing replacement, after which the process must be repeated on the same already-compromised teeth. Over a 30 to 40 year period, the total cost and clinical impact of a bridge often exceeds that of an implant placed well at the outset.
When is a bridge the right choice?

Bridges remain a valid option in certain situations — for example, when there is insufficient bone for an implant without significant grafting, when the adjacent teeth already require crowns for other reasons, or where budget is a primary consideration. Your dentist will advise based on your specific clinical situation.

title: All-on-4 dental implants explained — what you need to know
excerpt: All-on-4 gives patients with significant tooth loss a fixed, non-removable full arch of teeth on just four implants. Here is how it works.

content.text: All-on-4 — also known as the Toronto bridge — is a full-arch dental implant solution that replaces an entire upper or lower set of teeth using just four strategically placed implants. For patients who have lost most or all of their teeth, or who are facing significant tooth loss, it offers a fixed, non-removable result without the limitations of dentures.
Why four implants?

Traditional full-arch implant treatment often required six or more implants per arch. The All-on-4 technique uses four implants, with the two rear implants placed at an angle to maximise contact with available bone. This angular placement allows the procedure to work even in patients with some degree of bone loss, often avoiding the need for extensive bone grafting.
What does the final result look like?

The final prosthesis is a full-arch bridge — a complete set of fixed teeth that attaches to the four implants. Unlike a denture, it does not come out. Patients eat, speak, and smile with it in place, and clean it much like natural teeth. It feels and functions significantly more like natural teeth than any removable solution.
Am I suitable for All-on-4?

Suitability depends on a thorough clinical assessment including a 3D scan. Most patients with good general health are candidates, though certain conditions — including uncontrolled diabetes, active gum disease, or significant bone loss in specific areas — may require additional treatment beforehand. The assessment at Apex Dental is free when you proceed with treatment and includes a 3D CBCT scan.
How long does treatment take?

In most cases, the four implants are placed in a single surgical appointment. A provisional bridge is often fitted the same day or shortly after. The final permanent bridge is typically completed after osseointegration — the period when the implants fuse with the bone — which takes around three to four months.
Learn more about dental implants at Apex Dental

title: How long do dental implants last — and how do you make them last longer?
excerpt: With proper care, a well-placed dental implant can last a lifetime. Here is what the evidence says and what you can do to protect your investment.

content.text: One of the most common questions patients ask before implant treatment is: how long will it last? The honest answer is that a well-placed dental implant, properly maintained, can last a lifetime. The titanium implant itself — the root component placed into the jaw — has an excellent long-term track record in clinical studies. The crown on top may eventually need replacing, typically after 15 to 25 years depending on the material and the forces placed on it.
What determines implant longevity?

Several factors influence how long an implant lasts. The quality of the initial planning and placement matters enormously — proper 3D imaging, correct positioning, and the choice of implant system all contribute to long-term stability. Patient factors also play a role: bone density, gum health, smoking status, and whether conditions like diabetes are well controlled.
Does smoking affect implants?

Yes, significantly. Smoking impairs blood supply to the gums, slows the healing process, and increases the risk of peri-implantitis — a form of gum disease that affects the tissue around implants. Smokers have a higher rate of implant complications and failures. Stopping smoking before and after implant placement significantly improves outcomes.
How to maximise the lifespan of your implants

Brush twice daily with a soft or electric toothbrush, clean between the implant and adjacent teeth with interdental brushes or floss, and attend regular hygiene appointments. Implants do not decay, but the gum and bone around them can be damaged by plaque if not managed. Routine maintenance is the single most important factor in long-term implant success.
What is peri-implantitis?

Peri-implantitis is an inflammatory condition that affects the gum and bone around an implant, caused by bacterial buildup. If left untreated, it can lead to bone loss and eventually implant failure. It is treatable if caught early — which is why regular professional hygiene visits matter even after implant placement.

title: Can I get dental implants if I have bone loss?
excerpt: Many patients are told they do not have enough bone for implants. The reality is more nuanced — and options exist even in challenging cases.

content.text: Bone loss in the jaw is a common concern among patients considering dental implants — particularly those who have been missing teeth for some time. When a tooth root is absent, the bone beneath it gradually resorbs. The longer a tooth has been missing, the more bone may have been lost. This leads some patients to believe implants are no longer an option for them. In many cases, this is not true.
Why does bone volume matter for implants?

A dental implant is a titanium post placed into the jawbone. For it to integrate successfully, there needs to be sufficient bone around it — in terms of both height and width. If bone volume is too low, the implant may not achieve the stability needed for long-term success. However, the threshold for what is considered sufficient has improved with advances in implant design and surgical technique.
What is bone grafting?

Bone grafting involves adding bone material to a deficient area to regenerate volume before or during implant placement. The graft material may come from the patient, from a donor, or from synthetic sources. Over several months, the graft integrates with the existing bone, creating a stronger foundation for the implant. It adds time and some cost to the overall treatment but makes implants possible for patients who would otherwise not be candidates.
Zygomatic implants and All-on-4 for severe bone loss

For patients with significant bone loss in the upper jaw, zygomatic implants — which anchor into the cheekbone rather than the jaw — can be an option. In many cases of bone loss, the angled placement used in the All-on-4 technique allows implants to be placed in areas of denser existing bone, avoiding the need for large-scale grafting entirely.
The only way to know is a 3D scan

Every bone loss situation is different. A 3D CBCT scan provides a precise picture of bone volume, density, and anatomy. At Apex Dental, this scan is included at no extra cost when you proceed with implant treatment. Only after this assessment can a clinician tell you definitively what your options are.

title: Dental implants vs dentures — an honest comparison
excerpt: Dentures are familiar. Implants are different. Here is a clear breakdown of the practical differences to help you make the right choice.

content.text: For patients facing significant tooth loss, dentures have long been the default solution. Dental implants offer a fundamentally different approach. The right choice depends on your clinical situation, priorities, and long-term goals — but understanding the practical differences clearly is the first step.
Stability and comfort

Conventional dentures rest on the gum surface and are held in place by suction or adhesive. They can move during eating and speaking, and many patients find lower dentures particularly unstable. Implant-supported restorations are fixed to the bone. They do not move. Patients can eat the same foods they ate with natural teeth and speak without the anxiety of a denture shifting.
Bone preservation

This is one of the most significant differences. When a tooth root is missing, the jaw bone beneath it gradually resorbs. Dentures sit on top of the gum and do not stimulate the bone at all — in fact, the pressure of a denture can accelerate bone loss over time. Implants integrate into the bone and stimulate it the same way natural roots do, preserving the jaw structure long-term.
Maintenance and daily life

Dentures are removed for cleaning and typically at night. Implant-supported teeth are cleaned in place — brushing and interdental cleaning, just like natural teeth. There are no adhesives, no soaking solutions, and no risk of leaving them behind.
Cost over time

Dentures have a lower upfront cost but need to be replaced regularly — typically every five to eight years — and may require relining as the bone beneath them changes shape. Implants have a higher initial investment but, with proper care, last significantly longer. Over 20 to 30 years, implants often represent better value.
The middle ground: implant-retained dentures

For patients who want improved stability without the full cost of a fixed bridge, implant-retained dentures offer a compromise. Two to four implants are placed, and the denture clips onto them for much greater security than conventional dentures — while still being removable for cleaning.

title: What is osseointegration — and why it is the key to implant success
excerpt: The reason dental implants feel like natural teeth comes down to a biological process called osseointegration. Here is what it means for your treatment.

content.text: One word comes up repeatedly in discussions about dental implants: osseointegration. It is the biological process that makes implants work — and understanding it helps explain why implants behave so differently from any other tooth replacement option.
What does osseointegration mean?

Osseointegration describes the direct structural and functional connection between living bone and the surface of a titanium implant. Over a period of several weeks to months after placement, the bone cells grow into and around the textured surface of the implant, anchoring it firmly in the jaw. This is not simply the bone healing around a foreign object — it is a genuine integration at a cellular level.
Why does it matter?

Osseointegration is what gives implants their stability and longevity. A fully integrated implant is held in the jaw as securely as a natural tooth root — it does not rely on adhesive, adjacent teeth, or mechanical retention. It also allows the implant to transmit biting forces directly to the bone, which stimulates the jaw and prevents the bone loss that follows tooth extraction.
How long does it take?

The osseointegration period typically takes three to four months, though this varies depending on bone density, the location in the jaw, and the patient's overall health. During this time, a temporary crown or provisional restoration is usually placed. The final crown is fitted only after integration is confirmed.
What can disrupt osseointegration?

Several factors can impair the process: smoking significantly reduces blood supply to the healing tissue, uncontrolled diabetes affects bone healing, and certain medications can influence bone metabolism. Following post-operative instructions, avoiding smoking, and attending follow-up appointments all support a successful outcome.

title: What happens during dental implant surgery — a step by step guide
excerpt: Many patients feel anxious about the implant procedure because they do not know what to expect. Here is exactly what happens, from first consultation to final crown.

content.text: Dental implant treatment involves several stages spread over a number of months. Understanding each stage in advance reduces anxiety and helps patients plan for the time involved. Here is a clear walkthrough of the full process at Apex Dental.
Stage 1: Consultation and 3D scan

The first appointment involves a thorough clinical examination and a 3D CBCT scan. This scan gives a detailed three-dimensional view of your bone volume, density, and anatomy. At Apex Dental, the consultation is free when you proceed with treatment, and the scan is included at no extra cost with implant surgery. The clinician uses this information to plan the precise position, angle, and depth of each implant.
Stage 2: Implant placement

The implant surgery is carried out under local anaesthesia. A small incision is made in the gum, a carefully sized channel is prepared in the bone, and the titanium implant is placed. The gum is then closed with sutures. The procedure typically takes 30 to 90 minutes depending on the number of implants. Most patients report the experience as more manageable than they expected.
Stage 3: Healing and osseointegration

Over the following three to four months, the implant integrates with the surrounding bone. During this period, some mild soreness and swelling in the first few days is normal. A temporary restoration may be placed to maintain aesthetics and function while healing takes place. Patients can eat normally, though avoiding very hard foods on the implant side is advised initially.
Stage 4: Abutment and final crown

Once integration is confirmed, the abutment — a small connector piece — is attached to the implant. Impressions or digital scans are taken to fabricate the final crown. The crown is then fitted and adjusted for bite. The result is a natural-looking, fully functional tooth that is cared for just like natural teeth.
Is it painful?

The procedure itself is carried out under local anaesthesia and should be entirely pain-free. Post-operative discomfort is typically mild to moderate and is managed with standard over-the-counter pain relief for the first two to three days. Most patients return to normal activities the following day.

title: Replacing a single missing tooth with a dental implant — what to expect
excerpt: A single missing tooth can affect your bite, your confidence, and the health of neighbouring teeth. A single implant is often the best long-term solution.

content.text: Losing a single tooth — whether through trauma, decay, or extraction — is more significant than it might initially seem. Left untreated, the gap can affect how you chew, cause neighbouring teeth to drift, accelerate bone loss in the jaw, and affect the long-term stability of adjacent teeth. A single dental implant addresses all of these issues.
Why not just leave the gap?

The teeth on either side of a gap will gradually tilt into the space over time. The tooth above or below the gap may also drift. This changes the bite, creates new areas that are difficult to clean, and increases the risk of decay and gum problems in the surrounding teeth. The jawbone beneath the gap also begins to resorb without a root to stimulate it.
The advantage of a single implant over a bridge

A single-tooth implant replaces the missing tooth completely without affecting the adjacent teeth. A bridge, by contrast, requires the teeth on either side to be permanently reduced and crowned to support the artificial tooth. If those adjacent teeth are healthy, this represents a significant sacrifice of healthy tooth structure. An implant avoids this entirely.
What does a single implant involve?

A single implant treatment involves a consultation and 3D scan, surgical placement of the titanium implant, a healing period of three to four months for osseointegration, and finally the placement of an abutment and custom crown. The crown is colour-matched to your natural teeth and sits flush with the gumline for a completely natural appearance.
How soon after extraction can I have an implant?

In some cases, an implant can be placed immediately after extraction — this is called immediate implant placement. In other cases, it is preferable to allow the socket to heal for a period first. The timing depends on the condition of the bone and gum at the extraction site. Your dentist will advise the most appropriate approach for your situation at the consultation.
